Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper repeatedly refers to UGT76G1 as a UDP-glucose-dependent glycosyltransferase/glycosyltransferase, supporting UDP-glycosyltransferase 76G1.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is given anywhere in the paper.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper explicitly places UGT76G1 in the biosynthetic pathway of steviol glycosides (Fig. 1b and text).

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show UGT76G1 performs β(1–3) glucose addition at both R1 and R2 ends of steviol glycosides.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The Results and structural analysis explicitly state that UGT76G1 transfers glucose from UDPG to the 3-hydroxyl of glucose A on steviol glycosides, creating a β(1–3) linkage at R1 or R2.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper directly tests UDP-glucose with steviolbioside, Reb A, ST, Reb D, Reb E, Rubu, and two self-made monoglucosides; all require steviol aglycone plus glucose A with free 3-hydroxyl.

Primary_Product.reason: Results identify products Reb B, Reb A, Reb I, Reb D, Reb M, and Rubu-derived RX and RY from UGT76G1 reactions.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper explicitly highlights Reb D and Reb M as desirable trace steviol glycosides and shows UGT76G1 forms Reb D from Reb E and Reb M from Reb D.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Steviol glycosides are described as diterpenoid steviol glycosides.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Introduction states steviol glycosides are natural zero-calorie sweeteners and that Reb D/Reb M have improved sweetness quality with reduced bitter aftertaste.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper states steviol glycosides are found in S. rebaudiana leaves and specifically mentions abundance in leaves.

Applied_Species.reason: The study context is Stevia, specifically Stevia rebaudiana.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly names Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni.

Source_Species.reason: UGT76G1 is described as being from S. rebaudiana and its encoding region was synthesized for expression.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Methods state the encoding region of UGT76G1 of S. rebaudiana was synthesized.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: UGT76G1 directly forms Reb D and Reb M in the tested pathway steps and also forms related intermediates/products by β(1–3) glucosylation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The enzyme acts after initial glucose addition, elaborating steviol glycosides at R1 and R2; this is a downstream glycosylation step.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper presents UGT76G1 as important for formation of desirable sweeteners, but does not experimentally establish it as a control step; the statement correctly says not explicitly established as rate-limiting.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show UGT76G1 promotes formation of preferred-taste β(1–3)-glucosylated steviol glycosides, including steps to Reb D and Reb M, and that R1 transfer is faster than R2.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are present in the paper: recombinant enzyme assays, HPLC, high-resolution MS/MS, kinetics, X-ray crystallography, and H25A/H25N mutagenesis.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper explicitly states UGT76G1 is a GT-B fold glycosyltransferase; as a UGT it is consistent with family 1 UDP-dependent glycosyltransferase.

Omics_Data.reason: PDB accession codes 6INF, 6ING, 6INH, and 6INI are explicitly listed in Data availability.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Results and Methods describe in vitro assays with UDP-glucose, crystallization with UDP and steviol glucosides, and report no significant effect of Mg2+ addition or EDTA treatment.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper directly shows R2 glucosylation is slower than R1 and does not report organism-level tradeoffs.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Introduction/Discussion explicitly mention genetically modified S. rebaudiana and microbial production of Reb D/Reb M, and suggest engineering opportunities based on hydrophobic recognition.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summarized points are directly supported by Results/Discussion: β(1–3) glucosylation with UDPG, action at both R1 and R2 with R1 favored, hydrophobic recognition and essential His25, and relevance to Reb D/Reb M plus artificial acceptor glycosylation.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Each claim matches the paper: no activity on free glucose/sophorose/steviol aglycone; requirement for hydrophobic aglycone and free 3-OH glucose; normal/flipped binding models; economic importance of R2 sugars and improved sweetness without bitterness is discussed.

corrections.reason: No EC number is given anywhere in the paper.

Enzyme_Name.reason: UGT85C2 is explicitly named in the pathway figure/text as a UGT enzyme; the expanded form UDP-glycosyltransferase is consistent with the nomenclature used in the paper.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided for UGT85C2.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: UGT85C2 is explicitly placed in the steviol glycoside biosynthetic pathway in Fig. 1b and text.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper states UGT85C2 places the first glucose at the C13-hydroxyl (R1) position.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Introduction explicitly says UGT85C2 places the first glucose with a β-1 glycosidic linkage at the C13-hydroxyl (R1) position of the steviol aglycone.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Methods state pure steviol aglycone was converted by UGT85C2 under the same reaction condition of UGT76G1; UDPG is the pathway sugar donor described in the paper.

Primary_Product.reason: Methods state the product had a single glucose at the R1 position of the steviol aglycone.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: UGT85C2 is part of steviol glycoside biosynthesis, but this paper does not directly test or quantify final nutrient products resulting specifically from UGT85C2 activity beyond generating a monoglucoside substrate.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The pathway context is steviol glycosides, a diterpenoid glycoside class.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Steviol glycosides are described as natural zero-calorie sweeteners, but this study does not directly connect UGT85C2 experimentally to final sweetener output in Results.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Steviol glycosides are stated to occur in leaves of S. rebaudiana, the pathway context for UGT85C2.

Applied_Species.reason: The pathway enzymes discussed are from Stevia.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly discusses Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni.

Source_Species.reason: UGT85C2 is presented as a Stevia pathway enzyme and was expressed/purified in this study for substrate preparation.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Within the pathway figure/text these enzymes are identified in Stevia rebaudiana.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: It is logically an upstream precursor-supplying step, but the paper does not directly demonstrate UGT85C2 contribution to final sweetener accumulation beyond making a test substrate.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper explicitly states UGT85C2 places the first glucose, i.e., an upstream initial glycosylation step.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The entry states not established, and indeed the paper does not establish rate limitation for UGT85C2.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Methods/Results support that UGT85C2 generated the R1 monoglucoside precursor used to show a single glucose at R1 is sufficient for further UGT76G1 action.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Methods explicitly state UGT85C2 was expressed and purified and used for enzymatic synthesis of a self-made steviol monoglucoside tested by UGT76G1.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: As a UGT, family 1 UDP-dependent glycosyltransferase is plausible, but this paper does not explicitly assign UGT85C2 to a protein family/domain.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: UGT85C2 was tested in vitro to synthesize the monoglucoside substrate.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a reasonable inference from pathway reconstruction, but the paper does not specifically discuss engineering applications for UGT85C2.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are supported at the level presented in this paper: UGT85C2 is assigned to initial R1 glucosylation, was used to synthesize an R1 monoglucoside in this study, and that product was further glucosylated by UGT76G1.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper indeed uses UGT85C2 mainly as a supporting enzyme for substrate preparation, not as the main mechanistic focus.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Supplying precursor for downstream R2 β(1–3) glucosylation is consistent with pathway logic, but not directly demonstrated as an end-product connection for UGT74G1 in this paper.
